Title: The molecular biology of fruity and floral aromas in beer and other alcoholic beverages

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Ale, wine, sake, lager strains discussed; no single focal variety
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: General metabolism genes can indirectly tune aroma output by adjusting precursor pools, redox, pH, glycolysis, acetyl-CoA availability, and stress physiology. Use cautiously in breeding/engineering because effects are pleiotropic and strain-dependent; best deployed with pathway-specific aroma genes and validated in industrial fermentation backgrounds.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Modulates multiple fruity/floral aroma metabolites indirectly through global metabolic state rather than a single dedicated aroma step.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: QTL/polygenic mapping; allele replacement; overexpression/deletion in some cited studies; fermentation aroma profiling
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Anaerobiosis, oxygen supply, sugar/high-gravity wort, nitrogen source, unsaturated fatty acids, hydrostatic CO2 pressure, fermentation temperature
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Not established for this gene set in the review
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Context-dependent general metabolic genes active during fermentative growth
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: QTL/polygenic mapping studies referenced; no accession numbers provided in review
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Aminodeoxychorismate synthase required for folate production; cited as a general effector of flavor synthesis rather than a dedicated aroma enzyme.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad metabolic perturbation may affect growth and overall aroma balance.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABZ1 affects flavor production indirectly through folate-linked metabolism. 2. It is considered a general effector rather than a dedicated fruity/floral aroma gene. 3. Its influence is likely pleiotropic and context dependent.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Fruity and floral beer aroma compounds
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Sensory fruitiness and floral notes in beer and alcoholic beverages

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May be targeted to alter intracellular pH and membrane electrochemical gradient, thereby modifying flavor metabolism globally; use only with caution due to essential housekeeping role.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirectly alters multiple aroma compounds by changing pH homeostasis and membrane energization.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: QTL/polygenic mapping and fermentation phenotype association cited in review
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fermentation stress, anaerobiosis, CO2 pressure, sugar content
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutive essential membrane ATPase during growth/fermentation
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Genetic mapping studies cited; no accession numbers
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified by the review as a general regulator of flavor-related metabolism rather than a dedicated aroma pathway gene.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely severe pleiotropic effects on growth and stress tolerance.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PMA1 is a major determinant of intracellular pH and electrochemical gradient. 2. Through global physiology it can influence flavor biosynthesis. 3. It is not a dedicated fruity aroma biosynthetic enzyme.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Fruity and floral beer aroma compounds

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be used to modulate glycolytic flux and thereby precursor supply for higher alcohols and esters, but should be paired with pathway genes because of broad metabolic consequences.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirectly affects broad aroma output via glycolytic regulation.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Sugar availability, fermentative growth, anaerobic fermentation
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Genetic mapping studies cited; no accession numbers
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Trehalose-6-phosphate synthase noted as global regulator of glycolysis influencing flavor metabolism indirectly.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential effects on growth, stress response, and carbon partitioning.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TPS1 controls glycolytic state. 2. This alters supply of precursors and cofactors feeding aroma pathways. 3. Effects are indirect and pleiotropic.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Fruity and floral beer aroma compounds

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful to alter acetyl-CoA supply for acetate ester production, but ACS2 is often the dominant fermentative acetyl-CoA source under anaerobic/high sugar conditions; stacking with ATF1 or downstream ester genes is more practical.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Influences acetate ester formation by affecting acetyl-CoA supply.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway analysis and cited fermentation genetics studies
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Aerobic vs anaerobic growth; glucose repression; non-fermentable carbon sources
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: ACS1 induced on non-fermentable carbon sources and aerobic conditions; repressed by glucose
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: ACS2; PDH-bypass enzymes PDC and ALD
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes ACS1 as part of acetyl-CoA formation and as a general effector; under brewing-like anaerobic high sugar conditions ACS2 predominates.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Context dependence may limit utility under industrial anaerobic fermentation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACS1 contributes to acetyl-CoA formation. 2. Acetyl-CoA availability influences acetate ester synthesis. 3. ACS1 is most active aerobically/on non-fermentable carbon and less central under high-glucose anaerobic brewing than ACS2.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Acetate esters such as isoamyl acetate and ethyl acetate

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: General effector of metabolism and aroma; overexpression can increase fruitier profiles in some media but responses are medium-dependent. Better used with BAT2/ATF1 or targeted selections.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Affects higher alcohols and overall aroma profile through branched-chain amino acid transamination and broader metabolism.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Medium composition; logarithmic growth; nitrogen and amino acid availability
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in logarithmic phase; repressed in stationary phase
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: BAT2; ILV pathway enzymes; Ehrlich pathway enzymes
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although also a direct pathway enzyme, the review lists BAT1 among general effectors because its influence extends broadly across metabolism depending on conditions.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Medium-dependent effects; altered amino acid metabolism can shift multiple aroma and growth traits.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAT1 participates in Ehrlich and branched-chain amino acid metabolism. 2. Manipulation changes higher alcohol output and aroma profile. 3. Its effect depends strongly on medium and growth phase.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes alcoholic, vinous, sweet, banana-like aroma context

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can tune aromatic higher alcohol output, but broad metabolic pleiotropy and condition dependence require strain-specific validation.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirect and direct effects on higher alcohols and overall aroma profile.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Stationary phase; medium composition; nitrogen source
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed during stationary phase; repressed during logarithmic growth
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Often the major BCAAT in synthetic medium fermentations, but role is context dependent.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May shift broad aroma balance and amino acid metabolism.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAT2 strongly affects higher alcohol synthesis. 2. It can dominate in some fermentation media. 3. Effects are context dependent and not restricted to one aroma compound.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A general metabolic gene that can modulate flavor under some conditions, but not a primary target for precise aroma engineering.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Condition-dependent indirect effect on aroma metabolism.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed by the review among genes known in specific metabolic pathways that may affect general metabolism and flavor depending on experimental conditions.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPD2 can influence aroma production indirectly. 2. Effects depend on metabolic context. 3. It is not presented as a dedicated fruity/floral pathway gene.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential secondary target for aroma modulation but not a primary deterministic locus according to this review.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirect, condition-dependent impact on overall aroma metabolism.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: General effector only; mechanistic detail not elaborated in this review.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AAD6 may affect flavor depending on conditions. 2. Role is indirect/general. 3. Not established as a principal pathway determinant here.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential secondary target for tuning global metabolism; requires empirical validation.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirect condition-dependent effects on aroma profile.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed as a possible general metabolic effector.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AAD10 can influence flavor in some contexts. 2. Role is indirect/general. 3. Not a central dedicated fruity/floral gene in this review.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential secondary target only; broad metabolic changes expected.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirect condition-dependent effect on aroma output.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as a gene involved in specific pathways but with possible broader metabolic effects.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AAD14 is a possible general effector of aroma metabolism. 2. Evidence is contextual. 3. It is not highlighted as a dedicated core biosynthetic determinant.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Secondary tuning target for amino acid-derived flavor metabolism; should be validated in production strain and medium.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirect global effect on aroma metabolism in addition to possible pathway-specific roles.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed among genes affecting general metabolism depending on conditions.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HOM1 may modulate flavor through general metabolism. 2. Role is condition dependent. 3. Not a dedicated key aroma determinant in this review.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Thi3/Kid1 influences higher alcohol production and can be targeted, but because it also behaves as a broader metabolic effector in cited studies, deployment should be strain-specific and balanced with sensory outcomes.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Affects higher alcohol formation, particularly isoamyl alcohol, and contributes to overall aroma profile.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical pathway studies; fermentation phenotype associations
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functions as decarboxylase in isoamyl alcohol formation but also listed as general metabolism effector in the review.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can elevate fusel alcohols, affecting heavy/alcoholic notes.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. THI3/KID1 contributes to α-ketoacid decarboxylation in higher alcohol synthesis. 2. It influences overall aroma. 3. Increased flux may also raise fusel alcohol burden.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native promoters in review unless otherwise stated
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Various beer, wine, sake and laboratory strains
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Ehrlich pathway / branched-chain amino acid catabolism
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful downstream reductase target for higher alcohol formation, but redundancy with multiple ADHs means single-gene engineering may have modest effects; better combined with upstream flux genes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces fusel aldehydes such as isoamyl aldehyde or phenylacetylaldehyde to the corresponding higher alcohols using NADH/NADPH-dependent alcohol dehydrogenase activity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports formation of higher alcohols contributing to fruity/floral/alcoholic beer aroma.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical pathway assignment and mutant studies cited for ADH family roles
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate higher alcohol products from aldehydes
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fermentative growth; redox state; oxygen availability
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of several redundant dehydrogenases reducing aldehydes in fusel alcohol synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered ethanol/fusel alcohol balance
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Higher alcohols including isoamyl alcohol, isobutanol, 2-phenylethanol
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADH1 contributes to the final reduction step of the Ehrlich pathway. 2. This step yields aroma-active higher alcohols. 3. Functional redundancy limits its uniqueness as a control point.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes alcoholic, vinous, fruity and floral notes

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Secondary reductase target; redundancy limits standalone utility.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces aldehyde intermediates to higher alcohols.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports higher alcohol production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway assignment
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of redundant dehydrogenase set.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can affect broader alcohol metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADH2 contributes to reduction of aldehydes to higher alcohols. 2. It acts redundantly with other ADHs. 3. It is not defined as a bottleneck step.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential mitochondrial reductase contributor within compartmentalized engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces fusel aldehydes to higher alcohols in mitochondrial context.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports higher alcohol synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway assignment
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included among dehydrogenases contributing to higher alcohol formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential mitochondrial redox effects
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADH3 participates in aldehyde reduction to fusel alcohols. 2. Mitochondrial localization links it to compartmentalized metabolism. 3. It is not singled out as a key bottleneck.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Minor redundant target.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces fusel aldehydes to alcohols.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports higher alcohol production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway assignment
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Redundant contributor.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad alcohol metabolism shifts
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADH4 contributes to reduction of fusel aldehydes. 2. It acts redundantly. 3. Not identified as decisive control step.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Minor redundant target.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces fusel aldehydes to higher alcohols.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports higher alcohol synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway assignment
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Redundant role with other ADHs and Sfa1.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broader alcohol metabolic changes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ADH5 participates in final aldehyde-to-alcohol conversion. 2. It is part of a redundant enzyme set. 3. No key control role established.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Constitutive overexpression used in some cited studies
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Beer, sake, wine and laboratory strains
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Premier engineering target for boosting fruity acetate esters, especially isoamyl acetate; combine with precursor-supplying genes and fermentor management. Avoid overexpression extremes because ethyl acetate and solvent-like notes also increase.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to higher alcohols or ethanol, producing acetate esters such as isoamyl acetate and ethyl acetate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Major promoter of banana-like isoamyl acetate and other acetate esters; overexpression can increase isoamyl acetate ~4-fold and ethyl acetate ~6-fold.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; overexpression; deletion background studies; kinetic analysis; fermentation metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Anaerobiosis, oxygen, unsaturated fatty acids, C/N ratio, high gravity, CO2/hydrostatic pressure, temperature, pitching rate
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: No classic epigenetic mechanism reported; transcription responds to physiological regulatory networks and oxygen/lipid signals
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low basal expression in brewing yeast, peaking 12–36 h during active anaerobic growth/fermentation
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Rap1; Rox1; Mga2; Gcr1; Gcn4; Oye2 (metabolic interaction via shared monoterpenoid substrate pool)
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: QTL/polygenic mapping cited; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Atf1 is localized to lipid droplets budding from ER and has broad substrate specificity, including monoterpene alcohol acetylation. Regulation is highly complex and central to brewing aroma.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excess ethyl acetate/solvent-like character; may upset aroma balance.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Isoamyl acetate; ethyl acetate; phenylethyl acetate; monoterpene acetate esters
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Higher alcohols or ethanol plus acetyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ATF1 is the major enzyme responsible for most acetate ester formation in yeast. 2. Its expression level is often more important than alcohol substrate availability for ester output. 3. Manipulation strongly alters beer fruitiness but can also raise solvent-like ethyl acetate.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Isoamyl acetate and other acetate esters
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Banana, pineapple, fruity and floral aroma

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Laboratory, beer, sake and wine strains
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for moderate ester enhancement when ATF1 overexpression would be too strong; attractive for balancing fruitiness without excessive solvent character.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to alcohol substrates, producing acetate esters.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Minor contributor to isoamyl acetate and ethyl acetate production compared with ATF1.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression studies in multiple yeast backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Minor role under typical fermentation conditions
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: ATF1; Say1 in sterol detoxification context
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Overexpression in wild-type industrial strains has subtler effects than ATF1; also implicated in sterol detoxification cycle.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Still may increase solvent-like notes if overused, though generally less than ATF1
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ATF2 is a paralog of ATF1 with lower contribution to fermentation esters. 2. Overexpression can elevate isoamyl acetate and ethyl acetate. 3. It offers a gentler engineering option than ATF1.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native or overexpression in cited studies
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Ehrlich pathway / branched-chain amino acid catabolism
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target to increase branched-chain higher alcohols and derivative esters; overexpression can create fruitier profile but may also elevate heavy fusel notes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transaminates branched-chain amino acids and corresponding α-ketoacids, interconverting leucine/isoleucine/valine with their ketoacid precursors in Ehrlich and anabolic pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls production of higher alcohols from branched-chain amino acid metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Deletion/overexpression; fermentation metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in logarithmic phase; low in stationary phase
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Relative contribution versus BAT2 depends strongly on medium; overexpression yielded fruitier profile.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Branched-chain amino acid-derived higher alcohol branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: More fusel alcohols can create heavier alcoholic aroma
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Branched-chain α-ketoacids or amino acids depending on direction
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important control point but condition dependent
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAT1 participates in Ehrlich pathway precursor generation. 2. Manipulating BAT1 changes higher alcohol concentrations and aroma profile. 3. Contribution is medium and growth-phase dependent.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Isoamyl alcohol, active amyl alcohol, isobutanol
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Banana-like, vinous, sweet, alcoholic notes and ester precursors

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native or overexpression in cited studies
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Ehrlich pathway / branched-chain amino acid catabolism
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key cytosolic target for altering branched-chain higher alcohol output; useful for fruitier fermentations but must be balanced to avoid excessive fusels.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transaminates branched-chain amino acids and corresponding α-ketoacids in the cytosol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Major determinant of higher alcohol formation in some synthetic media and stationary-phase conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Deletion/overexpression; fermentation aroma profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Medium composition; stationary phase; nitrogen source
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in stationary phase; low in log phase
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Often major BCAAT in synthetic medium; overexpression increases fruitiness.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Branched-chain amino acid-derived higher alcohol branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increased fusel alcohol load
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important control point but context dependent
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAT2 strongly influences higher alcohol production. 2. It often dominates in synthetic medium fermentation. 3. Overexpression yields fruitier profiles but may raise fusels.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Isoamyl alcohol, active amyl alcohol, isobutanol

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can support terpenoid precursor enhancement in de novo engineered aroma yeast, usually in multi-gene stacks.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Synthesizes geranylgeranyl diphosphate / trans-prenyl intermediates in isoprenoid metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to terpenoid precursor metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway assignment from review table
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed as terpenoid backbone gene in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May perturb sterol/isoprenoid balance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BTS1 participates in terpenoid backbone synthesis. 2. It supports precursor supply rather than direct flavor-forming step. 3. Useful mainly in multi-gene terpenoid engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful in synthetic biology stacks to elevate linalool, geraniol and related monoterpenoids, especially together with mutant ERG20.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes hexaprenyl pyrophosphate synthesis in coenzyme Q pathway and, when overexpressed with mutant ERG20, supports increased monoterpene production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances linalool, geraniol, α-terpineol and related terpenoids in engineered yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression engineering and metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a native dedicated monoterpene synthase but can improve flux toward monoterpenes in engineered backgrounds.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbation of native ubiquinone/isoprenoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COQ1 overexpression with mutant ERG20 raised monoterpene output. 2. It acts indirectly through isoprenoid precursor metabolism. 3. Useful in synthetic monoterpene-producing brewer's yeast.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Various strains; ortholog first isolated from Wickerhamomyces anomalus
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Alternative target to ATF1 for ethyl acetate tuning; strong strain dependency means allele testing in intended industrial background is essential.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to ethanol to form ethyl acetate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances ethyl acetate formation, especially under aerobic conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression; polygenic analysis; natural variant discovery
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Aerobic conditions; strong strain background effects
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: YGR015C ortholog noted; no accession given
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Natural loss-of-function allele eat1K179fs lowers ethyl acetate. Effects of alleles are highly strain dependent.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Too much ethyl acetate causes solvent-like aroma
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Brewer's yeast / Wickerhamomyces anomalus ortholog context
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EAT1 is an alternative ethanol acetyltransferase contributing to ethyl acetate. 2. Natural truncation alleles reduce ethyl acetate. 3. Engineering outcomes are highly strain dependent.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Fruity at moderate levels, solvent-like at high levels

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for increasing apple-like ethyl hexanoate, preferably together with medium-chain acyl-CoA supply engineering; substrate availability is the major bottleneck.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses ethanol with medium-chain acyl-CoA, notably hexanoyl-CoA, to form ethyl hexanoate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes apple-like ethyl hexanoate formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene characterization; metabolite profiling; comparison with EHT1
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression not strongly correlated with final ester level
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Eeb1 is particularly active among enzymes forming ethyl hexanoate.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Medium-chain fatty acid ethyl ester branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Depends on medium-chain fatty acid flux; may alter lipid balance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EEB1 forms ethyl hexanoate from ethanol and medium-chain acyl-CoA. 2. It is more active than EHT1 for this product. 3. Final production is mainly limited by substrate availability rather than transcript abundance.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Secondary target alongside EEB1 for ethyl hexanoate engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers medium-chain acyl group from acyl-CoA to ethanol to form ethyl hexanoate and related ethyl esters.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to ethyl hexanoate production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene characterization
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression does not strongly correlate with final ester level
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Less active than Eeb1 according to review.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Medium-chain fatty acid ethyl ester branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May perturb lipid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EHT1 contributes to ethyl hexanoate formation. 2. It is less active than EEB1. 3. Product levels are mainly substrate limited.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native; overexpression under ACT1 promoter in cited wine study
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising for releasing hop- or fruit-derived glycosidically bound terpenoids; overexpression can unlock floral/citrus aromas without adding exogenous enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes β-glucan and glycosidic bonds with non-specific β-glucosidase-like activity, releasing terpenoid aglycones from glycosides.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Raises free terpenoid aroma compounds and can indirectly alter yeast ester/higher alcohol profile.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Activity assays; strain comparisons; overexpression with aroma release measurements
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for release/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low oxygen/hypoxic fermentation represses native EXG1; glycoside-rich substrates increase relevance
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High during exponential growth; repressed in low hypoxic conditions during fermentation
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Kex2; Sec pathway components; Kre6 affects extracellular release
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Brewing Saccharomyces generally lacks strong BGL but high-EXG strains show BGL-like activity. Overexpression boosted terpenoid release from grape glycosides.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Glycoside hydrolysis of hop/fruit aroma precursors
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter multiple aroma classes unexpectedly
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Free terpenoid aglycones such as linalool
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EXG1 confers β-glucosidase-like activity toward hop/fruit glycosides. 2. Overexpression increases release of free terpenoid aroma compounds. 3. Native expression is weak under hypoxic fermentation, limiting aroma release.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major engineering lever for de novo monoterpene production; truncated, deregulated HMG1 is a standard flux booster in synthetic aroma yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces HMG-CoA to mevalonate, a committed early step of the mevalonate pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances monoterpene production when truncated/deregulated and combined with downstream synthases.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metabolic engineering; fermentation metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: ERG20K197E; plant monoterpene synthases in engineered strains
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Truncated Hmg1 lacking regulatory domain strongly correlated with total monoterpene output in engineered brewer’s yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can perturb sterol metabolism and growth
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HMG1 drives mevalonate pathway flux. 2. Truncated/deregulated forms boost monoterpene synthesis in engineered yeast. 3. It is a major upstream control point for synthetic hoppy aroma production.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Excellent target for increasing isoamyl acetate by reducing hydrolysis; loss-of-function can complement ATF1 activation and improve ester retention.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes isoamyl acetate and other acetate esters to the corresponding alcohol and acetate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduces acetate ester levels; loss of function significantly increases isoamyl acetate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning and characterization; loss-of-function studies
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fermentation conditions; selection with compounds affecting esterase/AATase ratio
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Balance between AATase and Iah1 esterase activities determines final acetate ester yield; purified Iah1 also showed reverse esterase activity only for ethyl hexanoate formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excess ester buildup may create unbalanced aroma
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Isoamyl acetate and other acetate esters
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IAH1 is the major isoamyl acetate hydrolase. 2. Loss of function increases isoamyl acetate and, to a lesser extent, other acetate esters. 3. Final ester abundance reflects the balance between Atf transferases and Iah1 esterase activity.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports engineered monoterpene production by balancing IPP/DMAPP supply; useful in stacked synthetic pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Isomerizes IPP to DMAPP, balancing C5 isoprenoid precursor pools.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports monoterpenoid biosynthesis and de novo engineered aroma production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway engineering studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed as key mevalonate-pathway gene for monoterpenoid synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Native isoprenoid balance disturbances
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IDI1 balances IPP and DMAPP. 2. This supports monoterpene precursor availability. 3. It is useful within multi-gene synthetic pathways rather than as a single decisive target.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native or overexpression in cited studies
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Wine and brewing yeasts; many natural strains contain truncated allele
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Polyfunctional thiol release from cysteinylated precursors
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: One of the best targets for enhancing tropical/grapefruit thiols from hop/grape precursors. Use full-length active allele and consider relieving nitrogen catabolite repression or subtelomeric silencing; monitor sulfur off-flavors in beer-scale trials.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cleaves carbon-sulfur bond of cysteine-S-conjugated thiol precursors, releasing free volatile thiols such as 4-methyl-4-sulfanyl-2-pentanone.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes release of high-impact polyfunctional thiols from non-volatile precursors.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression; substrate conversion assays; natural allele analysis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product from precursor
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Nitrogen catabolite repression; ammonium represses; favorable release under nitrogen limitation
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Subject to Sir2-dependent subtelomeric silencing via histone H3/H4 deacetylation; silencing defects increase IRC7 expression
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Repressed by favorable nitrogen sources; induced by nitrogen catabolite repression
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same; full-length allele more common in Saccharomyces sensu stricto species than many S. cerevisiae strains
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: URE2; Sir2; Sir3; Sir4; ECM38; OPT1; GAP1
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: About 75% of S. cerevisiae strains carry inactive 38-bp truncation. Telomeric position differs between species and may explain interspecies variation in thiol release.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential sulfur-related off-flavor risk needs fermentation testing, though not reported in wine trials discussed
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key bottleneck enzyme for specific precursors
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IRC7 is the main β-lyase releasing some hop/grape thiols from cysteine conjugates. 2. Activity is often limited by inactive natural alleles and by nitrogen catabolite repression. 3. Subtelomeric silencing and species background strongly influence expression and thiol release.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Powerful target for increasing isoamyl alcohol precursor flux; resistant alleles release feedback inhibition and raise banana-associated isoamyl alcohol/acetate. Must be paired with esterification tuning to avoid overly heavy fusel notes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses α-ketoisovalerate with acetyl-CoA and water to form α-isopropylmalate in leucine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased LEU4 flux elevates isoamyl alcohol and can enhance banana-like aroma via more isoamyl acetate precursor.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Feedback-resistant mutant selection with trifluoroleucine; fermentation metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leucine feedback inhibition; selection with leucine analog trifluoroleucine
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Long isoform mitochondrial; short isoform cytosolic from downstream start codon
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Feedback inhibited by leucine and free CoA via C-terminal regulatory domain; resistant mutants greatly elevate isoamyl alcohol in sake, wine and low-alcohol beer.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher isoamyl alcohol can cause heavy alcoholic aroma
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: α-Isopropylmalate synthase with regulatory C-terminal domain
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LEU4 controls entry into leucine biosynthesis feeding isoamyl alcohol production. 2. Feedback-resistant alleles strongly boost isoamyl alcohol output. 3. Increased precursor flux can improve banana esters but also raise fusel burden.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful supporting target for de novo terpenoid engineering as part of a pathway stack.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Decarboxylates diphosphomevalonate to isopentenyl diphosphate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports terpenoid precursor formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway assignment and engineering context in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Supports IPP biosynthesis in mevalonate route.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered sterol/isoprenoid balance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MVD1 forms IPP in the mevalonate pathway. 2. It supports monoterpene precursor supply. 3. Best used in multigene engineering frameworks.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target depending on desired outcome: overexpress to increase β-citronellol from geraniol, or delete to retain more geraniol. Particularly useful for shaping hop aroma profile rather than total aroma intensity.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces geraniol to β-citronellol using NADPH-dependent ene reductase activity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts geraniol into β-citronellol, altering citrus/floral balance of hop aroma.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene overexpression/deletion; fermentation metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early fermentation (first 2–4 days) especially relevant in late-hopped beers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Overexpression increased geraniol-to-citronellol conversion to 87%; deletion improved geraniol accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Shifts aroma ratio away from geraniol toward citronellol
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for geraniol→citronellol conversion
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OYE2 catalyzes geraniol reduction to β-citronellol. 2. Overexpression strengthens citronellol formation, while deletion preserves geraniol. 3. It is a major lever for tuning monoterpene aroma balance in beer.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target for ethyl octanoate enhancement; useful especially if validated together with acyl substrate supply alleles.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts as lysophospholipase with in vitro ethanol acyltransferase activity, contributing to ethyl octanoate formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strong effector of ethyl octanoate production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Polygenic analysis; gene disruption confirmation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified by polygenic analysis as strong effector of ethyl octanoate production.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May affect membrane lipid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important effector, not fully established as sole bottleneck
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PLB2 contributes to ethyl octanoate production. 2. Polygenic mapping and gene disruption confirmed a strong effect. 3. It links lipid metabolism to fruity ethyl ester formation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Secondary redundant reductase target; broad metabolic role limits precision.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces aldehyde intermediates to higher alcohols.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports higher alcohol production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway assignment
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included among enzymes with aldehyde reductase activity toward fusel aldehydes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broader aldehyde detoxification changes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SFA1 contributes to reduction of fusel aldehydes. 2. It is one member of a redundant reductase set. 3. No exclusive control role established.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supporting target for precursor engineering in monoterpene-producing yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Prenyl diphosphate synthase involved in terpenoid backbone biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports monoterpenoid precursor availability.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway assignment from review table
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed as monoterpenoid-related gene in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May perturb native isoprenoid homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SRT1 supports terpenoid backbone synthesis. 2. It acts upstream of flavor-active monoterpenoids. 3. Best considered a supporting engineering component.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Polyfunctional thiol release from cysteine conjugates
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful auxiliary target to enhance release of 3-sulfanylhexan-1-ol-type thiols, especially in combination with IRC7 and precursor transport genes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts primarily as cystathionine β-lyase but shows residual carbon-sulfur β-lyase activity toward cysteine-S-3-sulfanylhexan-1-ol precursor, releasing free thiol when overexpressed.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression elevates free 3-sulfanylhexan-1-ol release from conjugated precursors.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression and substrate conversion assays
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product from precursor
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Natural preferred substrate is not the thiol precursor, but overexpression reveals latent flavor-releasing capability.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential sulfur balance effects; beer-scale validation needed
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STR3 has residual β-lyase activity toward some thiol conjugates. 2. Overexpression significantly increases free thiol release. 3. It complements rather than replaces IRC7 in thiol engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Cultivars including Shinshu Wase and wild hops discussed
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key breeding target in hops for increasing monoterpene precursor supply and aroma potential; selection for high trichome expression can enhance monoterpene-rich cultivars.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses IPP and DMAPP to geranyl diphosphate, the direct precursor for monoterpene synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides monoterpene precursor pool in hop glands, enabling aroma-active monoterpene production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning from hop trichomes; heterologous expression in E. coli; enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Small subunit highly expressed in glandular trichomes and correlated with myrcene concentration; large subunit also in stems, leaves, flowers
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Small and large GPPS subunits; MTS enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: cDNA library and comparative RNA-seq mentioned; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Heterodimer forms both GPP and GGPP in vitro; large subunit alone favors GGPP more strongly.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Shifts in GPP/GGPP allocation may affect other isoprenoids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPPS provides GPP for monoterpene biosynthesis in hops. 2. Trichome expression is linked to aroma potential. 3. It is a key upstream breeding target for hop aroma improvement.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Monoterpenoids such as myrcene, linalool, geraniol-derived products
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Hop cones/glandular trichomes; later transferred to beer
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Floral, citrus, fruity and hoppy aroma

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Cultivated hop cv. Shinshu Wase among discussed varieties
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target for breeding high-aroma hop cultivars via elevated monoterpene synthase expression in cones/trichomes.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Monoterpene synthase producing hop monoterpenes from GPP; exact product spectrum not specified in review for MTS1.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with elevated monoterpene aroma metabolism in hop cones.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq and cDNA characterization context
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms target-product branch or immediate monoterpenes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher expression in hop cone of cultivated cv. Shinshu Wase than in wild hop
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative RNA-seq mentioned; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Interpretation in review suggests domestication selected for higher specialized aroma metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could alter overall terpene balance among compounds
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MTS1 is a hop monoterpene synthase linked to aroma metabolism. 2. Higher cone expression in cultivated hops suggests breeding selection for aroma. 3. It is a promising candidate for hop flavor improvement.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Direct target for increasing myrcene-rich hop varieties; useful when breeding for specific fresh-hop aroma profiles, while noting myrcene is often lost during brewing.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts GPP to myrcene.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces myrcene, a major hop monoterpenoid and aroma precursor for brewing.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; heterologous expression in E. coli; enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hop cultivar and trichome expression context
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not fully detailed; trichome/flower-derived cDNA source
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Myrcene is abundant in hops but largely does not persist directly into most beers.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: High myrcene in hops does not guarantee high beer myrcene due to losses during brewing
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MTS2 is the characterized myrcene synthase of hop. 2. It generates a major hop oil component. 3. Brewing losses mean hop myrcene abundance does not directly translate into finished beer aroma.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hop aroma contributor and precursor context

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for shaping hop essential oil composition, though these sesquiterpene hydrocarbons often persist poorly into beer and are more relevant for precursor profiles than direct finished-beer aroma.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts FPP into β-caryophyllene and α-humulene.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces major hop sesquiterpene hydrocarbons.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; heterologous expression in E. coli; enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Products are major hop oil constituents but are hydrophobic and often transformed or lost during brewing.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Direct beer aroma impact may be limited despite high hop content
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STS1 forms α-humulene and β-caryophyllene in hops. 2. These are major hop sesquiterpene hydrocarbons. 3. Their direct contribution to final beer aroma is limited by poor persistence and transformation during brewing.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hop aroma precursors / kettle-hop character context

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Secondary target for specialized sesquiterpene profile breeding in hops.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts FPP to β-elemene-related sesquiterpene products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports hop sesquiterpene diversity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA characterization context from review table
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Less emphasized than STS1 in final beer aroma discussion.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unclear direct impact on final beer aroma
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STS2 contributes to hop sesquiterpene biosynthesis. 2. It broadens essential oil diversity. 3. Specific effects on beer aroma are less defined than for monoterpene-rich pathways.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Heterologous candidate for engineering yeast capable of converting myrcene to linalool/geraniol, potentially creating new hoppy/floral beer profiles without reliance on hop transformation by native yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrates myrcene to linalool and catalyzes linalool/geraniol isomerization.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Potentially enables formation of linalool and geraniol from myrcene in engineered systems.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Bacterial enzyme purification/characterization; not demonstrated in yeast according to review
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Anaerobic myrcene biodegradation context in bacterium
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not applicable in yeast; not demonstrated
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: GEOA; GEOB in downstream bacterial pathway
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included by review as non-yeast microbial enzyme with relevance to terpenoid biotransformation possibilities.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Heterologous pathway may generate non-native aroma balances
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LDI interconverts myrcene, linalool and geraniol in bacteria. 2. The enzyme suggests routes not yet shown in yeast. 3. It is a promising heterologous candidate for beer aroma engineering.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native; superior nonsense allele identified in mapping
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Beer strains in polygenic mapping; various industrial strains
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Truncated/inactive TOR1 can boost 2-phenylethanol and 2-phenylethyl acetate via ARO9/ARO10 induction; promising breeding target for floral rose/honey aroma, but expect broad nitrogen-signaling pleiotropy.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Polygenic mapping; allele identification; fermentation phenotyping; consistency with rapamycin response
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller linking nitrogen/TOR signaling to aromatic amino acid catabolism and floral ester production
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Nitrogen source; TORC1 activity; rapamycin-like repression
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Participates in nutrient-responsive control of aromatic amino acid catabolism
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Aromatic amino acid catabolism / Ehrlich pathway transcription
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Superior allele was nonsense mutation E216* causing truncated likely inactive Tor1.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad stress/nutrient signaling effects; possible growth and global metabolism changes.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Reduced TOR1 activity promotes ARO9/ARO10 expression and aromatic fusel alcohol/ester production
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TOR1 is a major nutrient-signaling regulator of floral aroma production. 2. A truncated allele increases 2-phenylethanol/2-phenylethyl acetate. 3. It likely acts through derepression of ARO9 and ARO10 under reduced TORC1 signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Nitrogen availability; TORC1 signaling status; rapamycin

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for tuning aromatic amino acid-derived floral alcohols; activation can enhance ARO9/ARO10, though global amino acid regulation must be considered.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes 2-phenylethanol formation via induction of aromatic amino acid catabolic genes.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential activator of aromatic amino acid Ehrlich genes for floral higher alcohol production
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Nitrogen source; aromatic amino acid availability
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Likely feedforward activation in response to aromatic amino acid catabolic needs
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter aromatic amino acid homeostasis more broadly.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Zn2Cys6-like fungal transcription factor (family not specified in review)
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ARO80 activates ARO9 and ARO10. 2. This drives aromatic amino acid catabolism toward 2-phenylethanol. 3. It is a decisive positive regulator of floral fusel alcohol biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Aromatic amino acid status; TOR signaling context

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulation can enhance floral aroma pathway under amino acid starvation-like states, but strong pleiotropy makes direct deployment risky.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports induction of ARO9/ARO10 and aromatic higher alcohol formation under amino acid limitation.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller linking amino acid starvation to 2-phenylethanol pathway activation
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Amino acid starvation; uncharged tRNA accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Part of amino acid homeostasis feedback system
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Aromatic amino acid catabolism under starvation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Regulates ARO9/ARO10 through mechanisms involving TORC1 and uncharged tRNA binding.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Phosphorylation-mediated starvation signaling leading to transcriptional induction
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes expression of ARO9/ARO10 under amino acid starvation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GCN2 senses amino acid starvation. 2. It helps induce ARO9/ARO10 and thus 2-phenylethanol production. 3. Effects are strongly condition dependent and pleiotropic.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important conceptual target for breeding/engineering fruitiness, but essential multifunctionality limits direct editing. Better exploited via upstream inputs or interacting regulators affecting Rap1 occupancy at ATF1.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activates ATF1 transcription in anaerobic growth while participating in repression under some conditions; central to acetate ester output.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major switch integrating growth and stress signals into ATF1 expression, thereby controlling acetate ester production
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Anaerobiosis, unsaturated fatty acids, growth state, high gravity, low nitrogen indirectly
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: None classically reported for ATF1 regulation here, though Rap1 also recruits silencing factors at telomeres in other contexts
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: General growth-associated TF; availability changes with growth arrest and oxygen
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Integrates multiple feedforward and antagonistic interactions with growth/stress pathways
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Gcr1; Gcn4; Sir proteins; Rif proteins; Rox1-related promoter context
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Acetate ester biosynthesis via ATF1 transcription
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts as both activator and repressor depending on interacting partners; central but indirect integrator.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Essential roles in telomere biology and ribosomal gene regulation create major pleiotropy.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interacts with Sir/Rif at telomeres and with Gcr1/Gcn4 at promoters
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation/repression depending on partner proteins
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates ATF1 anaerobically; contributes to repression by unsaturated fatty acids via partner-dependent regulation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RAP1 is essential for ATF1 transcriptional control. 2. It links growth state and nutrient/stress signals to acetate ester synthesis. 3. Partner-dependent action explains the complex regulation of beer fruitiness.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Isoamyl acetate and other acetate esters
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Growth state; oxygen; nutrient status; stress-linked TF partners

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to derepress fruitiness, but oxygen-response function and broad hypoxic regulation require careful balancing.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Represses ATF1 under oxygen-rich conditions, lowering acetate ester production.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major oxygen-dependent repressor connecting aeration to suppressed acetate ester synthesis
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Oxygen abundance; aerobic vs anaerobic fermentation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced by Hap1 under oxygenated conditions
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Acetate ester biosynthesis via ATF1 repression
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Strong ATF1 repression by oxygen occurs mainly via Rox1.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering ROX1 may disrupt broader hypoxic gene regulation.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: HMG-box transcriptional repressor (family not specified in review)
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ROX1 is the main oxygen-responsive repressor of ATF1. 2. Oxygen-driven ROX1 activity lowers acetate ester synthesis. 3. It explains reduced fruitiness upon aeration.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising target for lifting unsaturated-fatty-acid repression of ATF1 and increasing isoamyl acetate; truncated alleles identified by non-GM selection in sake demonstrate applied value.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activation state influences ATF1 expression and acetate ester production; truncated allele increased isoamyl acetate ~2.6-fold in sake yeasts.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant selection (aureobasidin A resistance); transcriptional analysis
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major lipid/temperature-responsive controller of ATF1 and thus acetate ester fruitiness
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Unsaturated fatty acids, oxygen, low temperature
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Membrane-tethered inactive precursor in ER; activated by proteolytic cleavage under appropriate conditions
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Part of membrane/lipid homeostasis loop with OLE1
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Acetate ester biosynthesis via ATF1 and lipid homeostasis
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mga2, but not Spt23, is emphasized as major player for ATF1/unsaturated fatty acid response. Truncated Ser706* allele releases ATF1 from unsaturated-fatty-acid repression.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changes membrane lipid homeostasis and cold/oxygen responses.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates ATF1 and OLE1 in response to lipid/temperature conditions
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: ER membrane precursor; active form in nucleus
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MGA2 mediates unsaturated-fatty-acid and cold effects on ATF1. 2. Truncated MGA2 alleles can strongly increase isoamyl acetate. 3. It is a prime regulator linking membrane status to beer fruitiness.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Secondary lipid-regulatory target; less decisive than MGA2 for ATF1-related fruitiness.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Complements Mga2 in OLE1 regulation but less central to ATF1-mediated ester control.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic interaction studies referenced in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Supportive regulator of lipid-state sensing; not the main determinant of ester output
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: OLE1/lipid homeostasis with indirect effects on ester formation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Double deletion with MGA2 causes unsaturated fatty acid auxotrophy; single deletion less severe.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SPT23 complements MGA2 in unsaturated fatty acid regulation. 2. Its role in acetate ester control is secondary. 3. It mainly influences fruitiness indirectly through membrane lipid homeostasis.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Indirect target for fruitiness through oxygen/heme control; direct manipulation is risky because of broad respiratory regulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Through activation of ROX1, suppresses ATF1 and acetate esters when oxygen is abundant.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Regulatory pathway studies referenced in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Upstream oxygen/heme signal transducer that governs ester repression via Rox1
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Part of oxygen-responsive feedforward repression circuit
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts upstream of Rox1 in heme-mediated oxygen response.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad effects on respiratory and oxygen-responsive genes.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HAP1 senses oxygen via heme. 2. It activates ROX1, which represses ATF1. 3. This pathway lowers fruity acetate esters under aerobic conditions.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native; mutant selection by cerulenin resistance
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Truncated YAP1 alleles can raise isoamyl acetate; may be used in non-GM selection, but oxidative-stress regulation and off-target stress phenotypes require caution.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Truncated mutants increased isoamyl acetate up to 3-fold in wine fermentations.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Strong repressor-linked controller of acetate ester production through oxidative stress/O2 regulatory network
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Oxidative stress; acetic acid stress; oxygen-related responses
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Acetate ester biosynthesis indirectly via stress response
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Rox1 can repress FET4 through Yap1 interaction; yap1Δ increased ROX1 transcript levels in cited work, indicating complex interactions.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered oxidative stress tolerance and acetic acid survival.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Indirectly ATF1-related network; FET4 in cited mechanistic example
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Complex; truncated alleles relieve repression and increase acetate ester formation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YAP1 links oxidative stress signaling to ester production. 2. Truncated alleles increase isoamyl acetate substantially. 3. It likely acts through interaction with the Rox1-linked repression network.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Oxidative stress; acetic acid; oxygen-related stress

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Wine strains, potentially applicable to beer strains
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target to increase thiol release by relieving nitrogen catabolite repression of IRC7; promising for tropical hop aroma enhancement, but beer-scale sulfur balance must be checked.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances polyfunctional thiol release through increased IRC7 expression.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Natural mutation introduction; thiol release analysis
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major nutrient-responsive gatekeeper of thiol precursor conversion
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Nitrogen catabolite repression; ammonium represses thiol release
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Part of nitrogen catabolite repression homeostasis
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: IRC7 and nitrogen catabolite repression network
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Naturally occurring mutation enhanced release of polyfunctional thiols by affecting IRC7 regulation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential broader nitrogen metabolism effects; sulfur off-flavor risk should be tested in beer.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Relief of repression increases IRC7 expression and thiol release
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. URE2 controls nitrogen catabolite repression affecting IRC7. 2. Mutations can increase polyfunctional thiol release. 3. It is a practical regulator for enhancing tropical hop aroma from precursors.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Epigenetic derepression of IRC7 via subtelomeric silencing factors is a conceptual route to higher thiol release; practical breeding may instead select species/strains with weaker silencing or more distal IRC7 loci.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Represses IRC7 by subtelomeric silencing, lowering thiol release.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Silencing mutant effects on IRC7 expression cited in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Epigenetic repressor determining whether thiol-release gene IRC7 is transcriptionally accessible
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Sir2-dependent deacetylation of H3/H4 lysines silences IRC7; sir2 defects increase expression
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Silencing factor acting at subtelomeric regions
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: SIR3; SIR4; IRC7 locus; Rap1 in telomeric silencing context
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Polyfunctional thiol release via chromatin silencing of IRC7
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Species with IRC7 located farther from telomere may exhibit higher thiol release.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad telomeric silencing defects and genome-wide expression changes.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SIR2 epigenetically silences IRC7 near the telomere. 2. Loss of silencing increases IRC7 expression and thiol release potential. 3. Chromosomal context is therefore an important determinant of tropical aroma formation.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Conceptual epigenetic targets for unlocking thiol release; practical use limited by broad genome effects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Support repression of IRC7 and thereby lower thiol release.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Part of essential subtelomeric silencing machinery controlling access to IRC7
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Participates in Sir-dependent subtelomeric silencing of IRC7
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Polyfunctional thiol release via chromatin state
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mutations in SIR3 or SIR4 increase IRC7 expression.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SIR3 and SIR4 collaborate with SIR2 to silence IRC7. 2. Reduced silencing increases thiol-release gene expression. 3. Epigenetic control is an underappreciated determinant of aroma output.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful plant breeding/engineering targets for increasing glutathionylated thiol precursors in raw material, especially under UV/stress-associated aroma programs.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of glutathione-conjugated thiol precursors that later release tropical thiols during fermentation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro enzyme assays; correlation with stress-induced expression
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Key upstream precursor-forming enzymes determining how much volatile thiol can later be released by yeast
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: UV radiation, drought stress, Botrytis infection; high nitrogen also affects precursor formation though not via these GSTs
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced in grapevine leaves under UV, drought and Botrytis; correlated with precursor formation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: High nitrogen-induced precursor formation may involve other unidentified enzymes because VvGST3/4 transcripts were not induced in that condition.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Stress-related induction may affect broader grape physiology.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Not applicable; enzymes form precursors directly
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VvGST3 and VvGST4 form glutathione-conjugated thiol precursors in vitro. 2. Their expression correlates with UV/drought/Botrytis-induced precursor accumulation. 3. They determine upstream raw-material potential for tropical thiol release during fermentation.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Polyfunctional thiol precursors leading to 3-sulfanylhexanol-type thiols
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Grape leaves/berries as precursor pool; later beverages

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Excellent malting barley breeding target for reducing trans-2-nonenal off-flavor and improving shelf life, while also altering lactone formation; already deployed in brewery breeding programs.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: LOX-2 activity forms γ-nonalactone but also trans-2-nonenal off-flavor; LOX-negative barley increases flavor stability.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Breeding of LOX-negative lines; malting/beer quality observations
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major raw-material controller of lactone/off-flavor precursor oxidation during malting and mashing
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Germination during malting; mashing; heat lability of LOX
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: LOX-2 active during germination; LOX-1/2 noted in barley
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Oxidation of fatty acid precursors to lactones and aldehydes
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: LOX-negative barley constructed by brewery-associated breeding programs to improve shelf life.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May reduce some coconut/sweet lactone notes while improving stability.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Not applicable; acts enzymatically on fatty acid substrates
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Barley LOX-2 is active during germination and contributes to lactone and aldehyde precursor formation. 2. It generates both desirable γ-nonalactone and undesirable trans-2-nonenal. 3. LOX-negative barley improves beer shelf life and flavor stability.
